input.task: Compare traditional ML systems with LLM systems across model source, customization, artifacts, and latency.

content.answer_preview:
Traditional ML systems are trained in-house using labeled training data, requiring a higher initial investment for data collection and labeling (Page 70). On the other hand, LLM systems are pre-trained via API, reducing the need for extensive labeled data but relying on prompts or few-shot examples instead (Page 70).

final_answer:
Traditional ML systems are trained in-house using labeled training data, requiring a higher initial investment for data collection and labeling (Page 70). On the other hand, LLM systems are pre-trained via API, reducing the need for extensive labeled data but relying on prompts or few-shot examples instead (Page 70).

In terms of customization, traditional ML systems require feature engineering, which involves creating and selecting relevant features from raw data (Page 70). In contrast, LLM systems require prompt engineering, where the user provides prompts to guide the model's behavior (Page 70).

Artifacts produced by traditional ML systems are model files, while LLM systems produce prompt templates (Page 70). The inference cost for traditional ML systems is predictable, while it varies based on token count for LLM systems (Page 70). Latency for traditional ML systems is measured in milliseconds, while latency for LLM systems is measured in seconds (Page 70).

For more detailed information about system architectures, refer to pages 64 and 62 (Chunks 71 and 69 respectively). By the end of this module, you will also learn to distinguish between traditional ML and LLM-based system architectures (Chunk 4, Page 5).
